From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Class of naturally occurring chemical compounds

This article is about the class of chemical compounds. For the pharmaceutical company, see [Alkaloid (company)](/wiki/Alkaloid_(company) "Alkaloid (company)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Papaver_somniferum_2021_G4.jpg/250px-Papaver_somniferum_2021_G4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Papaver_somniferum_2021_G4.jpg)

The first individual alkaloid, [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), was isolated in 1804 from the [opium poppy](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum") (*Papaver somniferum*).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

**Alkaloids** are a broad class of [naturally occurring](/wiki/Natural_product "Natural product") [organic compounds](/wiki/Organic_compound "Organic compound") that contain at least one [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") atom. Some synthetic compounds of similar structure may also be termed alkaloids.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Alkaloids are produced by a large variety of organisms including [bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria"), [fungi](/wiki/Fungus "Fungus"), [plants](/wiki/Medicinal_plant "Medicinal plant"), and [animals](/wiki/Animal "Animal").[[3]](#cite_note-3) They can be purified from crude extracts of these organisms by [acid-base extraction](/wiki/Acid-base_extraction "Acid-base extraction"), or solvent extractions followed by silica-gel [column chromatography](/wiki/Column_chromatography "Column chromatography").[[4]](#cite_note-G._P._Fox_et_al_2013-4) Alkaloids have a wide range of [pharmacological](/wiki/Pharmacology "Pharmacology") activities including [antimalarial](/wiki/Antimalarial_medication "Antimalarial medication") (e.g. [quinine](/wiki/Quinine "Quinine")), [antiasthma](/wiki/Asthma "Asthma") (e.g. [ephedrine](/wiki/Ephedrine "Ephedrine")), [anticancer](/wiki/Chemotherapy "Chemotherapy") (e.g. [homoharringtonine](/wiki/Omacetaxine_mepesuccinate "Omacetaxine mepesuccinate")),[[5]](#cite_note-PCS2014-5) [cholinomimetic](/wiki/Cholinomimetic "Cholinomimetic") (e.g. [galantamine](/wiki/Galantamine "Galantamine")),[[6]](#cite_note-PAA2013-6) [vasodilatory](/wiki/Vasodilation "Vasodilation") (e.g. [vincamine](/wiki/Vincamine "Vincamine")), [antiarrhythmic](/wiki/Antiarrhythmic_agent "Antiarrhythmic agent") (e.g. [quinidine](/wiki/Quinidine "Quinidine")), [analgesic](/wiki/Analgesic "Analgesic") (e.g. [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine")),[[7]](#cite_note-morphine-7) [antibacterial](/wiki/Antibacterial "Antibacterial") (e.g. [chelerythrine](/wiki/Chelerythrine "Chelerythrine")),[[8]](#cite_note-TBA2014-8) and [antihyperglycemic](/wiki/Anti-diabetic "Anti-diabetic") activities (e.g. [berberine](/wiki/Berberine "Berberine")).[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) Many have found use in [traditional](/wiki/Traditional_medicine "Traditional medicine") or [modern medicine](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_drug "Pharmaceutical drug"), or as starting points for [drug discovery](/wiki/Drug_discovery "Drug discovery"). Other alkaloids possess [psychotropic](/wiki/Psychoactive_drug "Psychoactive drug") (e.g. [psilocin](/wiki/Psilocin "Psilocin")) and [stimulant](/wiki/Stimulant "Stimulant") activities (e.g. [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine"), [caffeine](/wiki/Caffeine "Caffeine"), [nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine "Nicotine"), [theobromine](/wiki/Theobromine "Theobromine")),[[11]](#cite_note-11) and have been used in [entheogenic](/wiki/Entheogenic "Entheogenic") rituals or as [recreational drugs](/wiki/Recreational_drug "Recreational drug"). Alkaloids can be [toxic](/wiki/Toxicity "Toxicity") (e.g. [atropine](/wiki/Atropine "Atropine"), [tubocurarine](/wiki/Tubocurarine "Tubocurarine")).[[12]](#cite_note-JMV1996-12) Although alkaloids act on a diversity of metabolic systems in humans and other animals, they almost uniformly evoke a [bitter taste](/wiki/Bitter_(taste)#Bitterness "Bitter (taste)").[[13]](#cite_note-Rhoades1979-13)

The boundary between alkaloids and other nitrogen-containing natural compounds is not clear-cut.[[14]](#cite_note-Meyers-14) Most alkaloids are [basic](/wiki/Base_(chemistry) "Base (chemistry)"), although some have neutral[[15]](#cite_note-GoldBook-15) and even weakly [acidic](/wiki/Acid "Acid") properties.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In addition to [carbon](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon"), [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") and [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen"), alkaloids may also contain [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") or [sulfur](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur"). Rarer still, they may contain elements such as [phosphorus](/wiki/Phosphorus "Phosphorus"), [chlorine](/wiki/Chlorine "Chlorine"), and [bromine](/wiki/Bromine "Bromine").[[17]](#cite_note-xumuk.ru-17) Compounds like [amino acid](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid") [peptides](/wiki/Peptide "Peptide"), [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein"), [nucleotides](/wiki/Nucleotide "Nucleotide"), [nucleic acid](/wiki/Nucleic_acid "Nucleic acid"), [amines](/wiki/Amine "Amine"), and [antibiotics](/wiki/Antibiotics "Antibiotics") are usually not called alkaloids.[[15]](#cite_note-GoldBook-15) Natural compounds containing nitrogen in the [exocyclic](/wiki/Alicyclic_compound "Alicyclic compound") position ([mescaline](/wiki/Mescaline "Mescaline"), [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin"), [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine"), etc.) are usually classified as [amines](/wiki/Amine "Amine") rather than as alkaloids.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Some authors, however, consider alkaloids a special case of amines.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-Aniszewski_110-21)

## Naming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Naming")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Meissner_alkalod_definition_article_1819.png/250px-Meissner_alkalod_definition_article_1819.png)](/wiki/File:Meissner_alkalod_definition_article_1819.png)

The article that introduced the concept of "alkaloid".

The name "alkaloids" (German: *Alkaloide*) was introduced in 1819 by German chemist [Carl Friedrich Wilhelm Meissner](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carl_Friedrich_Wilhelm_Mei%C3%9Fner "de:Carl Friedrich Wilhelm Meißner"), and is derived from late Latin root *alkali* and the Greek-language suffix -οειδής -('like').[[nb 1]](#cite_note-22) However, the term came into wide use only after the publication of a review article, by Oscar Jacobsen in the chemical dictionary of [Albert Ladenburg](/wiki/Albert_Ladenburg "Albert Ladenburg") in the 1880s.[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)

There is no unique method for naming alkaloids.[[24]](#cite_note-Hesse_5-25) Many individual names are formed by adding the suffix "ine" to the species or genus name.[[25]](#cite_note-26) For example, [atropine](/wiki/Atropine "Atropine") is isolated from the plant *[Atropa belladonna](/wiki/Atropa_belladonna "Atropa belladonna")*; [strychnine](/wiki/Strychnine "Strychnine") is obtained from the seed of the [Strychnine tree](/wiki/Strychnine_tree "Strychnine tree") (*Strychnos nux-vomica* L.).[[17]](#cite_note-xumuk.ru-17) Where several alkaloids are extracted from one plant their names are often distinguished by variations in the suffix: "idine", "anine", "aline", "inine" etc. There are also at least 86 alkaloids whose names contain the root "vin" because they are extracted from *[vinca](/wiki/Vinca "Vinca")* plants such as *Vinca rosea* (*[Catharanthus roseus](/wiki/Catharanthus_roseus "Catharanthus roseus")*);[[26]](#cite_note-27) these are called [*vinca* alkaloids](/wiki/Vinca_alkaloid "Vinca alkaloid").[[27]](#cite_note-CurrMedChem-VA-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Friedrich_Wilhelm_Adam_Sertuerner.jpg/250px-Friedrich_Wilhelm_Adam_Sertuerner.jpg)](/wiki/File:Friedrich_Wilhelm_Adam_Sertuerner.jpg)

[Friedrich Sertürner](/wiki/Friedrich_Sert%C3%BCrner "Friedrich Sertürner"), the German chemist who first isolated morphine from opium.

Alkaloid-containing plants have been used by humans since ancient times for therapeutic and recreational purposes. For example, medicinal plants have been known in [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") from about 2000 BC.[[30]](#cite_note-Aniszewski_182-31) The *[Odyssey](/wiki/Odyssey "Odyssey")* of Homer referred to a gift given to Helen by the Egyptian queen, a drug bringing oblivion. It is believed that the gift was an opium-containing drug.[[31]](#cite_note-32) A Chinese book on houseplants written in 1st–3rd centuries BC mentioned a medical use of [ephedra](/wiki/Ephedra_(medicine) "Ephedra (medicine)") and [opium poppies](/wiki/Opium_poppies "Opium poppies").[[32]](#cite_note-33) Also, [coca](/wiki/Coca "Coca") leaves have been used by Indigenous South Americans since ancient times.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

Extracts from plants containing toxic alkaloids, such as [aconitine](/wiki/Aconitine "Aconitine") and [tubocurarine](/wiki/Tubocurarine "Tubocurarine"), were used since antiquity for poisoning arrows.[[30]](#cite_note-Aniszewski_182-31)

Studies of alkaloids began in the 19th century. In 1804, the German chemist [Friedrich Sertürner](/wiki/Friedrich_Sert%C3%BCrner "Friedrich Sertürner") isolated from opium a "soporific principle" (Latin: *principium somniferum*), which he called "morphium", referring to [Morpheus](/wiki/Morpheus_(mythology) "Morpheus (mythology)"), the Greek god of dreams; in German and some other Central-European languages, this is still the name of the drug. The term "morphine", used in English and French, was given by the French physicist [Joseph Louis Gay-Lussac](/wiki/Joseph_Louis_Gay-Lussac "Joseph Louis Gay-Lussac").

A significant contribution to the chemistry of alkaloids in the early years of its development was made by the French researchers [Pierre Joseph Pelletier](/wiki/Pierre_Joseph_Pelletier "Pierre Joseph Pelletier") and [Joseph Bienaimé Caventou](/wiki/Joseph_Bienaim%C3%A9_Caventou "Joseph Bienaimé Caventou"), who discovered [quinine](/wiki/Quinine "Quinine") (1820) and [strychnine](/wiki/Strychnine "Strychnine") (1818). Several other alkaloids were discovered around that time, including [xanthine](/wiki/Xanthine "Xanthine") (1817), [atropine](/wiki/Atropine "Atropine") (1819), [caffeine](/wiki/Caffeine "Caffeine") (1820), [coniine](/wiki/Coniine "Coniine") (1827), [nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine "Nicotine") (1828), [colchicine](/wiki/Colchicine "Colchicine") (1833), [sparteine](/wiki/Sparteine "Sparteine") (1851), and [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine") (1860).[[34]](#cite_note-35) The development of the chemistry of alkaloids was accelerated by the emergence of [spectroscopic](/wiki/Spectroscopic "Spectroscopic") and [chromatographic](/wiki/Chromatographic "Chromatographic") methods in the 20th century, so that by 2008 more than 12,000 alkaloids had been identified.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

The first complete synthesis of an alkaloid was achieved in 1886 by the German chemist [Albert Ladenburg](/wiki/Albert_Ladenburg "Albert Ladenburg"). He produced [coniine](/wiki/Coniine "Coniine") by reacting 2-methylpyridine with [acetaldehyde](/wiki/Acetaldehyde "Acetaldehyde") and [reducing](/wiki/Oxidation-reduction_reaction "Oxidation-reduction reaction") the resulting 2-propenyl pyridine with sodium.[[36]](#cite_note-BSE:_koniin-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Bufotenin.svg/250px-Bufotenin.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bufotenin.svg)

[Bufotenin](/wiki/Bufotenin "Bufotenin"), an alkaloid from some toads, contains an [indole](/wiki/Indole "Indole") core, and is produced in living organisms from the amino acid [tryptophan](/wiki/Tryptophan "Tryptophan").

## Classifications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Classifications")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Nicotine.svg/250px-Nicotine.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nicotine.svg)

The [nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine "Nicotine") molecule contains both [pyridine](/wiki/Pyridine "Pyridine") (left) and [pyrrolidine](/wiki/Pyrrolidine "Pyrrolidine") rings (right).

Compared with most other classes of natural compounds, alkaloids are characterized by a great structural diversity. There is no uniform classification.[[38]](#cite_note-ref15-39) Initially, when knowledge of chemical structures was lacking, botanical classification of the source plants was relied on. This classification is now considered obsolete.[[17]](#cite_note-xumuk.ru-17)[[39]](#cite_note-40)

More recent classifications are based on similarity of the carbon skeleton (*e.g.*, [indole](/wiki/Indole "Indole")-, [isoquinoline](/wiki/Isoquinoline "Isoquinoline")-, and [pyridine](/wiki/Pyridine "Pyridine")-like) or biochemical precursor ([ornithine](/wiki/Ornithine "Ornithine"), [lysine](/wiki/Lysine "Lysine"), [tyrosine](/wiki/Tyrosine "Tyrosine"), [tryptophan](/wiki/Tryptophan "Tryptophan"), etc.).[[17]](#cite_note-xumuk.ru-17) However, they require compromises in borderline cases;[[38]](#cite_note-ref15-39) for example, [nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine "Nicotine") contains a pyridine fragment from [nicotinamide](/wiki/Nicotinamide "Nicotinamide") and a [pyrrolidine](/wiki/Pyrrolidine "Pyrrolidine") part from ornithine[[40]](#cite_note-41) and therefore can be assigned to both classes.[[41]](#cite_note-ref19-42)

Alkaloids are often divided into the following major groups:[[42]](#cite_note-43)

1. "True alkaloids" contain [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") in the [heterocycle](/wiki/Heterocycle "Heterocycle") and originate from [amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid").[[43]](#cite_note-ref21-44) Their characteristic examples are [atropine](/wiki/Atropine "Atropine"), [nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine "Nicotine"), and [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"). This group also includes some alkaloids that besides the nitrogen heterocycle contain [terpene](/wiki/Terpene "Terpene") (*e.g.*, [evonine](/w/index.php?title=Evonine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Evonine (page does not exist)")[[44]](#cite_note-45)) or peptide fragments (*e.g.* [ergotamine](/wiki/Ergotamine "Ergotamine")[[45]](#cite_note-ref23-46)). The piperidine alkaloids [coniine](/wiki/Coniine "Coniine") and [coniceine](/w/index.php?title=Coniceine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Coniceine (page does not exist)") may be regarded as true alkaloids (rather than pseudoalkaloids: see below)[[46]](#cite_note-ref24-47) although they do not originate from amino acids.[[47]](#cite_note-ref25-48)
2. "Protoalkaloids", which contain [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") (but not the nitrogen heterocycle) and also originate from amino acids.[[43]](#cite_note-ref21-44) Examples include [mescaline](/wiki/Mescaline "Mescaline"), [adrenaline](/wiki/Adrenaline "Adrenaline") and [ephedrine](/wiki/Ephedrine "Ephedrine").
3. [Polyamine](/wiki/Polyamine "Polyamine") alkaloids – derivatives of [putrescine](/wiki/Putrescine "Putrescine"), [spermidine](/wiki/Spermidine "Spermidine"), and [spermine](/wiki/Spermine "Spermine").
4. Peptide and [cyclopeptide](/wiki/Cyclopeptide "Cyclopeptide") alkaloids.[[48]](#cite_note-ref27-49)
5. Pseudoalkaloids – alkaloid-like compounds that do not originate from amino acids.[[49]](#cite_note-50) This group includes [terpene](/wiki/Terpene "Terpene")-like and [steroid](/wiki/Steroid "Steroid")-like alkaloids,[[50]](#cite_note-51) as well as [purine](/wiki/Purine "Purine")-like alkaloids such as [caffeine](/wiki/Caffeine "Caffeine"), [theobromine](/wiki/Theobromine "Theobromine"), [theacrine](/wiki/Theacrine "Theacrine") and [theophylline](/wiki/Theophylline "Theophylline").[[51]](#cite_note-ref30-52) Some authors classify [ephedrine](/wiki/Ephedrine "Ephedrine") and [cathinone](/wiki/Cathinone "Cathinone") as pseudoalkaloids. Those originate from the amino acid [phenylalanine](/wiki/Phenylalanine "Phenylalanine"), but acquire their nitrogen atom not from the amino acid but through [transamination](/wiki/Transamination "Transamination").[[51]](#cite_note-ref30-52)[[52]](#cite_note-ref31-53)

Some alkaloids do not have the carbon skeleton characteristic of their group. So, [galanthamine](/wiki/Galanthamine "Galanthamine") and homoaporphines do not contain [isoquinoline](/wiki/Isoquinoline "Isoquinoline") fragment, but are, in general, attributed to isoquinoline alkaloids.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

## Properties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Properties")]

Most alkaloids contain oxygen in their molecular structure; those compounds are usually colorless crystals at ambient conditions. Oxygen-free alkaloids, such as [nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine "Nicotine")[[160]](#cite_note-161) or [coniine](/wiki/Coniine "Coniine"),[[36]](#cite_note-BSE:_koniin-37) are typically volatile, colorless, oily liquids.[[161]](#cite_note-ref222-162) Some alkaloids are colored, like [berberine](/wiki/Berberine "Berberine") (yellow) and [sanguinarine](/wiki/Sanguinarine "Sanguinarine") (orange).[[161]](#cite_note-ref222-162)

Most alkaloids are weak bases, but some, such as [theobromine](/wiki/Theobromine "Theobromine") and [theophylline](/wiki/Theophylline "Theophylline"), are [amphoteric](/wiki/Amphoteric "Amphoteric").[[162]](#cite_note-ref225-163) Many alkaloids dissolve poorly in water but readily dissolve in [organic solvents](/wiki/Organic_solvent "Organic solvent"), such as [diethyl ether](/wiki/Diethyl_ether "Diethyl ether"), [chloroform](/wiki/Chloroform "Chloroform") or [1,2-dichloroethane](/wiki/1,2-dichloroethane "1,2-dichloroethane"). [Caffeine](/wiki/Caffeine "Caffeine"),[[163]](#cite_note-164) [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine"),[[164]](#cite_note-165) [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine")[[165]](#cite_note-166) and [nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine "Nicotine")[[166]](#cite_note-167) are slightly soluble in water (with a solubility of ≥1g/L), whereas others, including [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine")[[167]](#cite_note-168) and [yohimbine](/wiki/Yohimbine "Yohimbine")[[168]](#cite_note-169) are very slightly water-soluble (0.1–1 g/L). Alkaloids and acids form salts of various strengths. These salts are usually freely soluble in water and [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol") and poorly soluble in most organic solvents. Exceptions include [scopolamine](/wiki/Hyoscine_hydrobromide "Hyoscine hydrobromide") hydrobromide, which is soluble in organic solvents, and the water-soluble quinine sulfate.[[161]](#cite_note-ref222-162)

Most alkaloids have a bitter taste or are poisonous when ingested. Alkaloid production in plants appeared to have evolved in response to feeding by herbivorous animals; however, some animals have evolved the ability to detoxify alkaloids.[[169]](#cite_note-170) Some alkaloids can produce developmental defects in the offspring of animals that consume but cannot detoxify the alkaloids. One example is the alkaloid [cyclopamine](/wiki/Cyclopamine "Cyclopamine"), produced in the leaves of [corn lily](/wiki/Veratrum_californicum "Veratrum californicum"). During the 1950s, up to 25% of lambs born by sheep that had grazed on corn lily had serious facial deformations. These ranged from deformed jaws to [cyclopia](/wiki/Cyclopia "Cyclopia"). After decades of research, in the 1980s, the compound responsible for these deformities was identified as the alkaloid 11-deoxyjervine, later renamed to cyclopamine.[[170]](#cite_note-171)

## Distribution in nature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Distribution in nature")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Strychnos_nux-vomica_-_K%C3%B6hler%E2%80%93s_Medizinal-Pflanzen-266.jpg/250px-Strychnos_nux-vomica_-_K%C3%B6hler%E2%80%93s_Medizinal-Pflanzen-266.jpg)](/wiki/File:Strychnos_nux-vomica_-_K%C3%B6hler%E2%80%93s_Medizinal-Pflanzen-266.jpg)

[Strychnine tree](/wiki/Strychnine_tree "Strychnine tree"). Its seeds are rich in [strychnine](/wiki/Strychnine "Strychnine") and [brucine](/wiki/Brucine "Brucine").

Alkaloids are [generated](/wiki/Anabolism "Anabolism") by various living organisms, especially by [higher plants](/wiki/Vascular_plant "Vascular plant") – about 10 to 25% of those contain alkaloids.[[171]](#cite_note-172)[[172]](#cite_note-173) Therefore, in the past the term "alkaloid" was associated with plants.[[173]](#cite_note-Hesse_4-174)

The alkaloids content in plants is usually within a few percent and is inhomogeneous over the plant tissues. Depending on the type of plants, the maximum concentration is observed in the leaves (for example, [black henbane](/wiki/Black_henbane "Black henbane")), [fruits](/wiki/Fruit "Fruit") or [seeds](/wiki/Seed "Seed") ([Strychnine tree](/wiki/Strychnine_tree "Strychnine tree")), root (*[Rauvolfia serpentina](/wiki/Rauvolfia_serpentina "Rauvolfia serpentina")*) or bark ([cinchona](/wiki/Cinchona "Cinchona")).[[174]](#cite_note-175) Furthermore, different tissues of the same plants may contain different alkaloids.[[175]](#cite_note-176)

Beside plants, alkaloids are found in certain types of [fungus](/wiki/Fungus "Fungus"), such as [psilocybin](/wiki/Psilocybin "Psilocybin") in the fruiting bodies of the genus *[Psilocybe](/wiki/Psilocybe "Psilocybe")*, and in animals, such as [bufotenin](/wiki/Bufotenin "Bufotenin") in the skin of some toads[[24]](#cite_note-Hesse_5-25) and a number of insects, markedly ants.[[176]](#cite_note-:1-177) Many marine organisms also contain alkaloids.[[177]](#cite_note-178) Some [amines](/wiki/Amines "Amines"), such as [adrenaline](/wiki/Adrenaline "Adrenaline") and [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin"), which play an important role in higher animals, are similar to alkaloids in their structure and biosynthesis and are sometimes called alkaloids.[[178]](#cite_note-179)

## Extraction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Extraction")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Piperine_crystals.jpg/250px-Piperine_crystals.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piperine_crystals.jpg)

Crystals of [piperine](/wiki/Piperine "Piperine") extracted from [black pepper](/wiki/Black_pepper "Black pepper").

Because of the structural diversity of alkaloids, there is no single method of their extraction from natural raw materials.[[179]](#cite_note-Hesse_116-180) Most methods exploit the property of most alkaloids to be soluble in organic solvents[[4]](#cite_note-G._P._Fox_et_al_2013-4) but not in water, and the opposite tendency of their salts.

Most plants contain several alkaloids. Their mixture is extracted first and then individual alkaloids are separated.[[180]](#cite_note-ref236-181) Plants are thoroughly ground before extraction.[[179]](#cite_note-Hesse_116-180)[[181]](#cite_note-182) Most alkaloids are present in the raw plants in the form of salts of organic acids.[[179]](#cite_note-Hesse_116-180) The extracted alkaloids may remain salts or change into bases.[[180]](#cite_note-ref236-181) Base extraction is achieved by processing the raw material with alkaline solutions and extracting the alkaloid bases with organic solvents, such as 1,2-dichloroethane, chloroform, diethyl ether or benzene. Then, the impurities are dissolved by weak acids; this converts alkaloid bases into salts that are washed away with water. If necessary, an aqueous solution of alkaloid salts is again made alkaline and treated with an organic solvent. The process is repeated until the desired purity is achieved.

In the acidic extraction, the raw plant material is processed by a weak acidic solution (*e.g.*, [acetic acid](/wiki/Acetic_acid "Acetic acid") in water, ethanol, or methanol). A base is then added to convert alkaloids to basic forms that are extracted with organic solvent (if the extraction was performed with alcohol, it is removed first, and the remainder is dissolved in water). The solution is purified as described above.[[179]](#cite_note-Hesse_116-180)[[182]](#cite_note-183)

Alkaloids are separated from their mixture using their different solubility in certain solvents and different reactivity with certain reagents or by [distillation](/wiki/Distillation "Distillation").[[183]](#cite_note-184)

A number of alkaloids are identified from [insects](/wiki/Insect "Insect"), among which the [fire ant](/wiki/Fire_ant "Fire ant") [venom](/wiki/Venom "Venom") alkaloids known as [solenopsins](/wiki/Solenopsin "Solenopsin") have received greater attention from researchers.[[184]](#cite_note-185) These insect alkaloids can be efficiently extracted by solvent immersion of live fire ants[[4]](#cite_note-G._P._Fox_et_al_2013-4) or by centrifugation of live ants[[185]](#cite_note-186) followed by silica-gel chromatography purification.[[186]](#cite_note-187) Tracking and dosing the extracted solenopsin ant alkaloids has been described as possible based on their absorbance peak around 232 nanometers.[[187]](#cite_note-188)

## Biosynthesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Biosynthesis")]

Biological precursors of most alkaloids are [amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid"), such as [ornithine](/wiki/Ornithine "Ornithine"), [lysine](/wiki/Lysine "Lysine"), [phenylalanine](/wiki/Phenylalanine "Phenylalanine"), [tyrosine](/wiki/Tyrosine "Tyrosine"), [tryptophan](/wiki/Tryptophan "Tryptophan"), [histidine](/wiki/Histidine "Histidine"), [aspartic acid](/wiki/Aspartic_acid "Aspartic acid"), and [anthranilic acid](/wiki/Anthranilic_acid "Anthranilic acid").[[188]](#cite_note-Plemenkov_253-189) [Nicotinic acid](/wiki/Nicotinic_acid "Nicotinic acid") can be synthesized from tryptophan or aspartic acid. Ways of alkaloid biosynthesis are too numerous and cannot be easily classified.[[85]](#cite_note-Begley-86) However, there are a few typical reactions involved in the biosynthesis of various classes of alkaloids, including synthesis of [Schiff bases](/wiki/Schiff_bases "Schiff bases") and [Mannich reaction](/wiki/Mannich_reaction "Mannich reaction").[[188]](#cite_note-Plemenkov_253-189)

### Synthesis of Schiff bases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Synthesis of Schiff bases")]

Main article: [Schiff base](/wiki/Schiff_base "Schiff base")

Schiff bases can be obtained by reacting amines with ketones or aldehydes.[[189]](#cite_note-190) These reactions are a common method of producing C=N bonds.[[190]](#cite_note-Dewick_19-191)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Schiff_base_formation.svg/500px-Schiff_base_formation.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Schiff_base_formation.svg)

In the biosynthesis of alkaloids, such reactions may take place within a molecule,[[188]](#cite_note-Plemenkov_253-189) such as in the synthesis of piperidine:[[41]](#cite_note-ref19-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Schiff_base_formation_intramolecular.svg/250px-Schiff_base_formation_intramolecular.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Schiff_base_formation_intramolecular.svg)

### Mannich reaction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Mannich reaction")]

Main article: [Mannich reaction](/wiki/Mannich_reaction "Mannich reaction")

An integral component of the Mannich reaction, in addition to an amine and a [carbonyl](/wiki/Carbonyl "Carbonyl") compound, is a [carbanion](/wiki/Carbanion "Carbanion"), which plays the role of the nucleophile in the [nucleophilic addition](/wiki/Nucleophilic_addition "Nucleophilic addition") to the ion formed by the reaction of the amine and the carbonyl.[[190]](#cite_note-Dewick_19-191)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/46/Mannich.png)](/wiki/File:Mannich.png)

The Mannich reaction can proceed both intermolecularly and intramolecularly:[[191]](#cite_note-192)[[192]](#cite_note-193)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Mannich_reaction_intramolecular.svg/330px-Mannich_reaction_intramolecular.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Mannich_reaction_intramolecular.svg)

## Dimer alkaloids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Dimer alkaloids")]

In addition to the described above monomeric alkaloids, there are also [dimeric](/wiki/Dimer_(chemistry) "Dimer (chemistry)"), and even [trimeric](/wiki/Trimer_(chemistry) "Trimer (chemistry)") and [tetrameric](/wiki/Tetramer "Tetramer") alkaloids formed upon condensation of two, three, and four monomeric alkaloids. Dimeric alkaloids are usually formed from monomers of the same type through the following mechanisms:[[193]](#cite_note-194)

* [Mannich reaction](/wiki/Mannich_reaction "Mannich reaction"), resulting in, *e.g.*, voacamine
* [Michael reaction](/wiki/Michael_reaction "Michael reaction") (villalstonine)
* Condensation of aldehydes with amines (toxiferine)
* Oxidative addition of phenols (dauricine, tubocurarine)
* [Lactonization](/wiki/Lactone "Lactone") (carpaine).

There are also dimeric alkaloids formed from two distinct monomers, such as the *vinca* alkaloids [vinblastine](/wiki/Vinblastine "Vinblastine") and vincristine,[[27]](#cite_note-CurrMedChem-VA-28)[[135]](#cite_note-MoleculesReview-136) which are formed from the coupling of [catharanthine](/wiki/Catharanthine "Catharanthine") and [vindoline](/wiki/Vindoline "Vindoline").[[194]](#cite_note-195)[[195]](#cite_note-TopCurrChem-196) The newer [semi-synthetic](/wiki/Semisynthesis "Semisynthesis") chemotherapeutic agent [vinorelbine](/wiki/Vinorelbine "Vinorelbine") is used in the treatment of [non-small-cell lung cancer](/wiki/Non-small-cell_lung_cancer "Non-small-cell lung cancer").[[135]](#cite_note-MoleculesReview-136)[[196]](#cite_note-197) It is another derivative dimer of vindoline and catharanthine and is synthesised from [anhydrovinblastine](/wiki/Anhydrovinblastine "Anhydrovinblastine"),[[197]](#cite_note-198) starting either from [leurosine](/w/index.php?title=Leurosine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Leurosine (page does not exist)")[[198]](#cite_note-Anhydro-199)[[199]](#cite_note-200) or the monomers themselves.[[135]](#cite_note-MoleculesReview-136)[[195]](#cite_note-TopCurrChem-196)

## Biological role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Biological role")]

Alkaloids are among the most important and best-known [secondary metabolites](/wiki/Secondary_metabolites "Secondary metabolites"), i.e. biogenic substances not directly involved in the normal [growth](/wiki/Cell_growth "Cell growth"), [development](/wiki/Biological_development "Biological development"), or [reproduction](/wiki/Reproduction "Reproduction") of the organism. Instead, they generally mediate ecological [interactions](/wiki/Biological_interaction "Biological interaction"), which may produce a selective advantage for the organism by increasing its [survivability](/wiki/Survivability "Survivability") or [fecundity](/wiki/Fecundity "Fecundity"). In some cases their function, if any, remains unclear.[[200]](#cite_note-201) An early hypothesis, that alkaloids are the final products of [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") [metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism") in plants, as [urea](/wiki/Urea "Urea") and [uric acid](/wiki/Uric_acid "Uric acid") are in mammals, was refuted by the finding that their concentration fluctuates rather than steadily increasing.[[14]](#cite_note-Meyers-14)

Most of the known functions of alkaloids are related to protection. For example, [aporphine](/wiki/Aporphine "Aporphine") alkaloid [liriodenine](/wiki/Liriodenine "Liriodenine") produced by the [tulip tree](/wiki/Liriodendron_tulipifera "Liriodendron tulipifera") protects it from parasitic mushrooms. In addition, the presence of alkaloids in the plant prevents insects and [chordate](/wiki/Chordate "Chordate") animals from eating it. However, some animals are adapted to alkaloids and even use them in their own metabolism.[[201]](#cite_note-202) Such alkaloid-related substances as [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin"), [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") and [histamine](/wiki/Histamine "Histamine") are important [neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") in animals. Alkaloids are also known to regulate plant growth.[[202]](#cite_note-203) One example of an organism that uses alkaloids for protection is the *[Utetheisa ornatrix](/wiki/Utetheisa_ornatrix "Utetheisa ornatrix")*, more commonly known as the ornate moth. Pyrrolizidine alkaloids render these larvae and adult moths unpalatable to many of their natural enemies like coccinelid beetles, green lacewings, insectivorous hemiptera and insectivorous bats.[[203]](#cite_note-204) Another example of alkaloids being utilized occurs in the [poison hemlock moth](/wiki/Agonopterix_alstroemeriana "Agonopterix alstroemeriana") (*Agonopterix alstroemeriana).* This moth feeds on its highly toxic and alkaloid-rich host plant [poison hemlock](/wiki/Conium_maculatum "Conium maculatum") (*Conium maculatum*) during its larval stage. *A. alstroemeriana* may benefit twofold from the toxicity of the naturally occurring alkaloids, both through the unpalatability of the species to predators and through the ability of *A. alstroemeriana* to recognize *[Conium maculatum](/wiki/Conium_maculatum "Conium maculatum")* as the correct location for oviposition.[[204]](#cite_note-205) A [fire ant](/wiki/Fire_ant "Fire ant") [venom](/wiki/Venom "Venom") alkaloid known as [solenopsin](/wiki/Solenopsin "Solenopsin") has been demonstrated to protect queens of [invasive fire ants](/wiki/Red_imported_fire_ant "Red imported fire ant") during the foundation of new nests, thus playing a central role in the spread of this pest ant species around the world.[[205]](#cite_note-206)

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Applications")]

### In medicine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: In medicine")]

Medical use of alkaloid-containing plants has a long history, and, thus, when the first alkaloids were isolated in the 19th century, they immediately found application in clinical practice.[[206]](#cite_note-207) Many alkaloids are still used in medicine, usually in the form of salts widely used including the following:[[14]](#cite_note-Meyers-14)[[207]](#cite_note-208)

| Alkaloid | Action |
| --- | --- |
| [Ajmaline](/wiki/Ajmaline "Ajmaline") | [Antiarrhythmic](/wiki/Antiarrhythmic_agent "Antiarrhythmic agent") |
| [Emetine](/wiki/Emetine "Emetine") | [Antiprotozoal agent](/wiki/Antiprotozoal_agent "Antiprotozoal agent"), [emesis](/wiki/Emesis "Emesis") |
| [Ergot alkaloids](/wiki/Ergoline "Ergoline") | [Vasoconstriction](/wiki/Vasoconstriction "Vasoconstriction"), [hallucinogenic](/wiki/Psychedelic_drug "Psychedelic drug"), [Uterotonic](/wiki/Uterotonic "Uterotonic") |
| [Glaucine](/wiki/Glaucine "Glaucine") | [Antitussive](/wiki/Antitussive "Antitussive") |
| [Morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") | [Analgesic](/wiki/Analgesic "Analgesic") |
| [Nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine "Nicotine") | [Stimulant](/wiki/Stimulant "Stimulant"), [nicotinic acetylcholine receptor agonist](/wiki/Nicotinic_agonist "Nicotinic agonist") |
| [Physostigmine](/wiki/Physostigmine "Physostigmine") | Inhibitor of [acetylcholinesterase](/wiki/Acetylcholinesterase "Acetylcholinesterase") |
| [Quinidine](/wiki/Quinidine "Quinidine") | Antiarrhythmic |
| [Quinine](/wiki/Quinine "Quinine") | [Antipyretic](/wiki/Antipyretic "Antipyretic"), [antimalarial](/wiki/Antimalarial_medication "Antimalarial medication") |
| [Reserpine](/wiki/Reserpine "Reserpine") | [Antihypertensive](/wiki/Antihypertensive_drug "Antihypertensive drug") |
| [Tubocurarine](/wiki/Tubocurarine "Tubocurarine") | Muscle relaxant |
| [Vinblastine](/wiki/Vinblastine "Vinblastine"), [vincristine](/wiki/Vincristine "Vincristine") | [Antitumor](/wiki/Chemotherapy "Chemotherapy") |
| [Vincamine](/wiki/Vincamine "Vincamine") | [Vasodilating](/wiki/Vasodilation "Vasodilation"), [antihypertensive](/wiki/Antihypertensive_drug "Antihypertensive drug") |
| [Yohimbine](/wiki/Yohimbine "Yohimbine") | [Stimulant](/wiki/Stimulant "Stimulant"), [aphrodisiac](/wiki/Aphrodisiac "Aphrodisiac") |
| [Berberine](/wiki/Berberine "Berberine") | [Antihyperglycaemic](/wiki/Antihyperglycaemic "Antihyperglycaemic")[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) |

Many synthetic and semisynthetic drugs are structural modifications of the alkaloids, which were designed to enhance or change the primary effect of the drug and reduce unwanted side-effects.[[208]](#cite_note-209) For example, [naloxone](/wiki/Naloxone "Naloxone"), an [opioid receptor](/wiki/Opioid_receptor "Opioid receptor") [antagonist](/wiki/Receptor_antagonist "Receptor antagonist"), is a derivative of [thebaine](/wiki/Thebaine "Thebaine") that is present in [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium").[[209]](#cite_note-210)

### In agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: In agriculture")]

Prior to the development of a wide range of relatively low-toxic synthetic [pesticides](/wiki/Pesticide "Pesticide"), some alkaloids, such as salts of nicotine and [anabasine](/wiki/Anabasine "Anabasine"), were used as [insecticides](/wiki/Insecticide "Insecticide"). Their use was limited by their high toxicity to humans.[[210]](#cite_note-211)

### Use as psychoactive drugs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alkaloid&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Use as psychoactive drugs")]

Preparations of plants and fungi containing alkaloids and their extracts, and later pure alkaloids, have long been used as [psychoactive substances](/wiki/Psychoactive_drug "Psychoactive drug"). [Cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine"), [caffeine](/wiki/Caffeine "Caffeine"), and [cathinone](/wiki/Cathinone "Cathinone") are [stimulants](/wiki/Stimulant "Stimulant") of the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system").[[211]](#cite_note-212)[[212]](#cite_note-213) [Mescaline](/wiki/Mescaline "Mescaline") and many indole alkaloids (such as [psilocybin](/wiki/Psilocybin "Psilocybin"), [dimethyltryptamine](/wiki/Dimethyltryptamine "Dimethyltryptamine") and [ibogaine](/wiki/Ibogaine "Ibogaine")) have [hallucinogenic](/wiki/Hallucinogen "Hallucinogen") effect.[[213]](#cite_note-214)[[214]](#cite_note-215) [Morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") and [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine") are strong narcotic pain killers.[[215]](#cite_note-216)

There are alkaloids that do not have strong psychoactive effect themselves, but are [precursors](/wiki/Precursor_(chemistry) "Precursor (chemistry)") for semi-synthetic psychoactive drugs. For example, [ephedrine](/wiki/Ephedrine "Ephedrine") and [pseudoephedrine](/wiki/Pseudoephedrine "Pseudoephedrine") are used to produce [methcathinone](/wiki/Methcathinone "Methcathinone") and [methamphetamine](/wiki/Methamphetamine "Methamphetamine").[[216]](#cite_note-217) [Thebaine](/wiki/Thebaine "Thebaine") is used in the synthesis of many painkillers such as [oxycodone](/wiki/Oxycodone "Oxycodone").
